narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 50.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on key details from your profile, like your Year 1 school decile of 2, Year 3 decile of 1, middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 14.5, undergraduate GPA of 3.2, being female, Asian background, and planning full-time studies. Compared to typical students, your deciles are much lower than the averages of about 5.7 for Year 1 and 5.5 for Year 3, your LSAT is well below the usual 36.7, while your GPA is right around the average of 3.21. The model weighed these factors to forecast your bar exam outcome, and several stood out as the strongest influences. The biggest driver was your Year 3 decile, which significantly nudged the prediction toward failure since it's in the bottom tier relative to peers. Next in influence was your LSAT score, also pushing toward a fail prediction due to how low it is compared to most applicants. Your Year 1 decile followed closely, reinforcing that early academic ranking as a concern. Family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status had progressively smaller but still negative influences, all tilting slightly toward expecting a failure. None of your features strongly pulled in the direction of passing, making this a close but unfavorable call. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your LSAT score through targeted prep or a retake, as it's a highly influential factor you can realistically improve. Strengthening your overall academic performance, perhaps through additional coursework or study habits to mirror higher decile students, could also help shift future predictions. Keep in mind that with dedication to these areas, many students turn around similar profiles and succeed on the bar exam.
